planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–November in zone 9) in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Austrian winter pea is a fast-growing, winter-hardy cover crop that fixes 60–150 lbs nitrogen per acre and creates substantial biomass for spring incorporation.

directions: Broadcast or drill seeds in fall at 2–4 lbs per 1,000 sq ft in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; inoculate seeds with rhizobium inoculant before planting for maximum nitrogen fixation. Pisum sativum ssp. arvense (Austrian winter pea or field pea) is one of the premier cool-season cover crops for the Gulf South and Southeast — a winter-annual legume that germinates quickly in fall, grows vigorously through the mild zone 9 winter, and can be terminated and incorporated in spring to release 100 lbs or more of biologically fixed nitrogen per acre as green manure. Unlike garden peas, field peas are grown for soil improvement rather than harvest (though young shoots and pods are edible). They are commonly mixed with cereal rye or oats as a winter cover-crop cocktail: the pea climbs the grain stems, which support the vines; the grain adds carbon while the pea adds nitrogen; together they create a thick, weed-suppressing, moisture-retaining cover. Terminate by mowing or rolling before flowering if retaining seed is a concern. In zone 9, fall sowing from September through November is ideal; the plants may succumb to hard freezes below 18°F but survive typical zone 9 winters with ease.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Cover Crops
NRCS Plant Materials Program — Austrian Winter Pea

planting.note: Direct sow or allow to self-seed in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean to moderately fertile, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; burclover is a Mediterranean annual legume that volunteers readily in Gulf Coast lawns and gardens, acting as a winter-active nitrogen fixer before dying back in summer heat.

directions: Broadcast seeds in fall in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; burclover requires little management and often self-seeds prolifically once established. Medicago polymorpha (burclover or California burclover) is a small, sprawling, Mediterranean annual legume that has naturalized widely throughout the Gulf Coastal Plain, California, and mild-winter regions of North America. In zone 9 lawns and gardens, it is often considered a volunteer ground cover — germinating with fall rains, growing through the mild winter as a low, clover-like plant with small, three-part leaves and tiny yellow flowers, fixing nitrogen symbiotically with soil rhizobia, and dying back naturally when summer heat arrives. The seeds are distinctive small, spiny burs that adhere to clothing and animal fur. While sometimes considered a weedy nuisance, burclover is increasingly recognized as a useful volunteer cover crop and winter ground cover for no-till and regenerative gardeners: it adds nitrogen, prevents winter erosion, and dies back naturally without tillage. It tolerates drought, alkaline soil, and poor fertility well — making it especially useful in challenging sites.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Cover Crops
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Annual Clovers

planting.note: Plant container stock or hardwood cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, loamy soil in full sun; golden mombin is a fast-growing tropical tree well-suited to zone 10 and warm zone 9 coastal areas, producing abundant oblong golden-yellow fruits with a sweet-tart, mango-like flavor.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; golden mombin is fast-growing and may produce fruit within 3–4 years from cuttings. Spondias dulcis (golden mombin, ambarella, or June plum) is a Polynesian and Melanesian tropical fruit tree — widely cultivated throughout the Caribbean, Southeast Asia, and tropical Americas — producing oblong, golden-yellow, 2-to-4-inch fruits that ripen to orange-yellow with a fibrous central pit and flesh with a flavor combining mango, pineapple, and tart apple. The fruits are eaten fresh when ripe, or green (unripe) as a crisp, tart, mango-like condiment in curries, chutneys, and salads across Southeast Asian cuisines — particularly popular in Vietnam and Cambodia where unripe ambarella is eaten with salt, chili, and sugar. The tree is fast-growing and highly productive once mature, often requiring thinning of the heavy crop to prevent limb breakage; it is semi-deciduous in seasonal climates. The leaves are also edible as a vegetable in some Pacific Island cuisines. In zone 9, grow as a container plant or in the most frost-protected locations, bringing indoors or covering when frost threatens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Fruits
Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Spondias dulcis

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in part shade to full shade; cupuassu is an understory tree native to the Amazon rainforest that produces its remarkable cauliflorous fruits (borne directly on trunk and main branches) in dappled shade — do not plant in full sun, which causes leaf scorch.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in part to full shade; cupuassu requires consistent moisture and high humidity and will not tolerate any frost or prolonged dry periods. Theobroma grandiflorum (cupuassu) is a Brazilian Amazon rainforest relative of cacao (T. cacao) producing large, 8-to-12-inch, oblong, woody fruits borne directly on the trunk and main branches (cauliflorous), each containing 20–50 seeds surrounded by a thick, creamy-white, intensely fragrant pulp with a flavor combining chocolate, pineapple, banana, and guava — considered by many food scientists to be one of the most complex and delicious tropical flavors in the world. The pulp is used to make juices, ice creams, yogurts, and confectioneries throughout Brazil, where it is the second most important Amazonian fruit after açaí. The seeds can be fermented and processed into a chocolate-like product ('cupuaçu butter') and are the basis of a proprietary chocolate-substitute trademarked as 'cupulate.' Growing cupuassu outside zones 11–12 requires a greenhouse; at the frontier of zone 10b, protected microclimates may support growth. A rare and extraordinary specialty fruit for true tropical enthusiasts.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Fruits
Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Theobroma grandiflorum

planting.note: Sow fresh seeds in late summer or fall (seeds require cold stratification and lose viability rapidly — sow within weeks of harvest); plant in moist, humus-rich, deep, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; angelica is a biennial or monocarpic perennial native to cool, moist northern European riverbanks and requires shade and moisture in southern gardens.

directions: Sow fresh seeds in late summer or fall in moist, humus-rich, deep, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; seeds lose viability quickly — sow immediately after harvest. Angelica archangelica (garden angelica) is a magnificent biennial herb of Scandinavian origin — one of the most architecturally impressive herbs in cultivation, reaching 5–8 feet in its flowering year with hollow, bright-green, purple-tinged stems as thick as a wrist and enormous, compound umbrella-flower heads (3–6 inches across) of tiny yellow-green flowers beloved by beneficial insects, especially predatory wasps and hoverflies. The stems, leaves, roots, and seeds are all used in European cuisine and confectionery: the young stems are candied (crystallized angelica) as a traditional cake and pastry decoration; the leaves and seeds flavor aquavit, vermouth, Chartreuse, Bénédictine, and Cointreau liqueurs; the roots have been used medicinally for centuries in Scandinavian and central European folk medicine. The plant prefers cool, moist, shaded conditions and struggles in hot, humid southern summers — in zone 9, a shaded, moist spot with afternoon protection is essential. CAUTION: sap can cause phototoxic skin reactions.
